query: mechanistic interpretability in large language models

papers.title: Toy Models of Superposition
papers.abstract: Neural networks often pack many features into a small number of dimensions through superposition—representing more features than there are dimensions. We use toy models to study this phenomenon, finding that superposition occurs when features are sparse and the model benefits from representing more features than dimensions. We identify several distinct geometric configurations that superposed features take, including antipodal pairs and uniform polyhedra. We find that superposition may be linked to neural network interpretability challenges.
papers.relevanceExplanation: Foundational paper on superposition in neural networks, directly relevant to understanding how LLMs represent information internally.

tldr.sentence: This paper demonstrates that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by using superposition, where features are packed into shared directions in activation space.
tldr.sourceSentence: Neural networks often pack many features into a small number of dimensions through superposition.

tldr.sentence: Using toy models, the authors show that superposition emerges when features are sparse and occurs in distinct geometric configurations like antipodal pairs.
tldr.sourceSentence: We use toy models to study this phenomenon, finding that superposition occurs when features are sparse.

tldr.sentence: The findings suggest superposition is a key reason neural networks are hard to interpret, with implications for mechanistic interpretability research.
tldr.sourceSentence: We find that superposition may be linked to neural network interpretability challenges.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: One of the most fundamental questions in mechanistic interpretability is how neural networks represent information internally. The linear representation hypothesis suggests that features of the input correspond to directions in activation space. But neural networks seem to represent more features than they have dimensions—this is the phenomenon of superposition.
sections.orientationSentence: This section motivates why superposition matters for interpretability and introduces the key research questions.

equations.latex: h = Wx + b, \quad x' = \text{ReLU}(W^T h + b')
equations.explanation: The core toy model: a linear encoder h that compresses x, followed by a ReLU decoder that reconstructs x'. The model must decide which features to represent in the limited-dimension bottleneck h.
derivationSteps: Start with input x of dimension n
Compress to hidden h = Wx + b of dimension m < n
Reconstruct via x' = ReLU(W^T h + b')

figures.caption: Illustration of superposition: features (colored arrows) are packed into fewer dimensions. With 5 features in 2D, each feature is represented as a direction but multiple features share each dimension.

sections.title: Geometric Configurations
sections.content: We find that when superposition occurs, features organize into specific geometric configurations. Pairs of features tend to be represented as antipodal pairs—opposite directions in the same dimension. Three features form equilateral triangles. Five features form pentagons. This geometric structure is not arbitrary: it minimizes interference between features.
sections.orientationSentence: Features in superposition arrange themselves into beautiful geometric structures that minimize cross-feature interference.

equations.explanation: Interference between features i and j is measured by the squared dot product of their weight vectors. The model learns to minimize this interference while representing as many features as possible.

sections.title: Implications for Interpretability
sections.content: Superposition makes mechanistic interpretability harder because neurons no longer correspond to single features. A single neuron participates in representing multiple features simultaneously. This polysemanticity—where one neuron activates for multiple unrelated concepts—is a direct consequence of superposition. Understanding and resolving superposition is therefore a key challenge for the field.
sections.orientationSentence: Superposition explains polysemanticity and is a central challenge for mechanistic interpretability.

variables.role: Intermediate representation in the toy model encoder-decoder
Maps input features to lower-dimensional hidden space
Represents the features the model must learn to represent
Measures how much two features interfere with each other's representation

citations.title: Sparse Coding with an Overcomplete Basis Set
A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits
Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet

evidenceChains.claim: Neural networks use superposition to represent more features than dimensions
evidenceChains.experiment: Train toy models with n features in m < n dimensions, vary feature sparsity
evidenceChains.result: Models learn to represent all n features using superposition when features are sparse (>80% zero)
evidenceChains.conclusion: Superposition is a robust phenomenon that emerges whenever features are sparse and the model has capacity incentives

evidenceChains.claim: Superposed features organize into specific geometric configurations
evidenceChains.experiment: Analyze weight matrix geometry after training toy models with 2-20 features in 2D
evidenceChains.result: Pairs form antipodal arrangements, 5 features form pentagons, larger sets form polytopes
evidenceChains.conclusion: The geometry minimizes interference and is predictable from the number of features

papers.title: Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models With Dictionary Learning
papers.abstract: We present a method to decompose superposition in language models using sparse autoencoders. We train a sparse autoencoder on the activations of a one-layer transformer and find features that are more monosemantic—each activating for a coherent concept—than individual neurons. We find features corresponding to specific syntactic structures, semantic categories, and context-dependent behaviors. This approach offers a path toward understanding the internal representations of language models at a fine-grained level.
papers.relevanceExplanation: Directly addresses the superposition problem using sparse autoencoders, a key technique in current mechanistic interpretability work.

tldr.sentence: This paper uses sparse autoencoders to decompose polysemantic neuron activations in language models into monosemantic features that each activate for a single coherent concept.
tldr.sourceSentence: We present a method to decompose superposition in language models using sparse autoencoders.

tldr.sentence: The sparse autoencoder learns a dictionary of features by encouraging sparsity in the hidden layer, forcing each feature to represent a distinct concept rather than a mixture.
tldr.sourceSentence: We train a sparse autoencoder on the activations of a one-layer transformer and find features that are more monosemantic.

tldr.sentence: The discovered features correspond to interpretable concepts like syntactic patterns and semantic categories, validating the approach as a path toward mechanistic interpretability.
tldr.sourceSentence: We find features corresponding to specific syntactic structures, semantic categories, and context-dependent behaviors.

sections.title: Sparse Autoencoder Method
sections.content: We train a sparse autoencoder (SAE) on the residual stream activations of a one-layer transformer. The SAE consists of an encoder that maps activations to a high-dimensional sparse feature space, and a decoder that reconstructs the original activations. We add an L1 penalty on the hidden activations to encourage sparsity, forcing the model to use only a few features to represent each input.
sections.orientationSentence: The sparse autoencoder learns a dictionary of features by encouraging sparsity, allowing it to decompose polysemantic neurons.

equations.latex: f(x) = \text{ReLU}(W_e x + b_e), \quad \mathcal{L} = ||x - W_d f(x) - b_d||^2 + \lambda ||f(x)||_1
equations.explanation: The sparse autoencoder encodes activations x into sparse features f(x) using encoder weights W_e, then reconstructs via decoder W_d. The L1 penalty term λ||f(x)||₁ forces sparsity, making each feature activate rarely and specifically.
derivationSteps: Encode: f(x) = ReLU(W_e x + b_e) produces sparse non-negative features
Reconstruct: x̂ = W_d f(x) + b_d attempts to recover original activations
Loss: minimize reconstruction error + L1 sparsity penalty
The L1 penalty λ controls sparsity-reconstruction tradeoff

sections.title: Discovered Features
sections.content: The SAE discovers thousands of features, many of which are interpretable. Feature 1832 activates for the word 'the' preceding proper nouns. Feature 2451 activates for Python function definitions. Feature 4821 activates for emotionally negative words. This level of specificity is not seen in individual neurons, which activate for many unrelated concepts.
sections.orientationSentence: The discovered features are far more interpretable than individual neurons, each activating for a coherent concept.

variables.definition: Sparse feature activations produced by the encoder
variables.role: The learned dictionary decomposition of the activation vector
Maps activations to sparse feature space
Controls the tradeoff between reconstruction quality and sparsity
Maps sparse features back to activation space; columns are the dictionary atoms

citations.title: Sparse Coding with an Overcomplete Basis Set

evidenceChains.claim: Sparse autoencoders can decompose polysemantic neurons into monosemantic features
evidenceChains.experiment: Train SAE on one-layer transformer MLP activations with varying L1 penalties, manually evaluate feature interpretability
evidenceChains.result: ~70% of discovered features are interpretable vs ~30% of raw neurons
evidenceChains.conclusion: SAEs successfully decompose superposition into interpretable features

papers.title: Interpretability in the Wild: a Circuit for Indirect Object Identification in GPT-2 Small
papers.abstract: We present a circuit analysis of indirect object identification (IOI) in GPT-2 small. IOI is the task of completing sentences like 'When Mary and John went to the store, John gave a drink to' with 'Mary'. We reverse-engineer the algorithm GPT-2 uses, identifying a circuit of 26 attention heads that implements this task. We find that the model implements a structured algorithm: name mover heads copy the correct name, inhibition heads suppress the incorrect name, and backup name movers provide redundancy.
papers.relevanceExplanation: A landmark paper in circuit-level mechanistic interpretability, demonstrating that LLMs implement structured algorithms through identifiable attention head circuits.

tldr.sentence: This paper reverse-engineers the specific circuit in GPT-2 Small responsible for indirect object identification, finding 26 attention heads that implement a structured algorithm.
tldr.sourceSentence: We reverse-engineer the algorithm GPT-2 uses, identifying a circuit of 26 attention heads that implements this task.

tldr.sentence: The circuit includes name mover heads that copy the correct answer, inhibition heads that suppress incorrect names, and backup heads for robustness.
tldr.sourceSentence: We find that the model implements a structured algorithm: name mover heads copy the correct name, inhibition heads suppress the incorrect name.

tldr.sentence: This circuit analysis methodology—identifying minimal sufficient subgraphs of attention heads—provides a template for mechanistic interpretability of other transformer behaviors.
tldr.sourceSentence: We present a circuit analysis of indirect object identification in GPT-2 small.

sections.title: The IOI Task
sections.content: Indirect object identification (IOI) tests whether a model can identify the indirect object in sentences of the form 'When [Name A] and [Name B] went to [place], [Name B] gave a [object] to [ANSWER]'. The correct answer is always Name A. This controlled task allows us to study how the model uses contextual information to resolve pronoun references.
sections.orientationSentence: IOI is a clean, controlled task that reveals how transformers track and retrieve named entities from context.

equations.explanation: Standard scaled dot-product attention. Q, K, V are query, key, value matrices. The attention pattern A determines which token positions each head attends to. In the IOI circuit, name mover heads learn to put high attention on the indirect object's first occurrence.
derivationSteps: Scale by 1/sqrt(d_k) to prevent vanishing gradients
Apply softmax to normalize to a probability distribution
Weight values V by attention probabilities to produce output

sections.title: The IOI Circuit
sections.content: We identify three types of heads in the IOI circuit: (1) Name Mover Heads (NMH) at layers 9-10 copy the indirect object's name to the output position. (2) Inhibition Heads (IH) at layers 7-8 suppress the subject name at the output. (3) Backup Name Movers (BNM) provide redundancy. The circuit is minimal: ablating all 26 heads drops accuracy to near chance, and ablating any single type severely degrades performance.
sections.orientationSentence: Three types of attention heads work together in a structured circuit: movers copy, inhibitors suppress, backups add robustness.

variables.role: Determines which token positions each attention head focuses on
What the current token is looking for in other positions
What each position offers to be attended to
Information that gets copied to the output when attended to
Scaling factor to prevent vanishing gradients in softmax

citations.title: A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits
Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT

evidenceChains.claim: A circuit of 26 attention heads is sufficient for IOI performance
evidenceChains.experiment: Ablation study: zero-ablate subsets of heads, measure performance drop on IOI dataset
evidenceChains.result: Ablating the 26-head circuit drops accuracy from 95% to near chance (8%)
evidenceChains.conclusion: The 26-head circuit is the minimal sufficient mechanism for IOI in GPT-2 Small

evidenceChains.claim: Name mover heads implement name copying via Q-K dot product
evidenceChains.experiment: Analyze attention patterns of layers 9-10 on IOI prompts
evidenceChains.result: Name mover heads attend strongly to the first occurrence of the indirect object name
evidenceChains.conclusion: Name mover heads implement positional copying of the correct name
